steps.title: Spot the appositive and its requirement
steps.content: "A politician from South Carolina," is an appositive phrase that renames Robert B. Elliott. The signal: an appositive must be immediately followed by the noun it describes as the main subject of the independent clause.

steps.title: Apply the appositive rule
steps.content:
An opening appositive phrase ("A politician from South Carolina,") must be followed immediately by the noun it renames as the subject of the main clause. That noun is Robert B. Elliott.

A — "Elliott was elected to the US House of Representatives in 1870 by his constituents.": Elliott appears immediately after the comma as subject. Correct.

B — "was the year that Elliott was elected...": No subject follows the appositive — "was" is a verb with nothing before it, creating a fragment. Fails.

C — "constituents of Elliott elected him...": "Constituents" follows the comma, so the appositive would mislabel "constituents" as the politician from South Carolina. Fails.

D — "in 1870, his constituents elected Elliott...": "In 1870" follows the comma, again misplacing the appositive and leaving no proper subject noun immediately after it. Fails.
